I don't know if it's powerful enough to cut steel, but if it is, that would be a good way to make a really thin version of the device. Most viewers might not be able to reproduce it without such a tool, but it would still be nice to watch it being made and seeing the end result.

author: @HypoceeYT
text:
Aside from the corrosion of the soul that the "Hur hur just use pencil dumy" story poses - slandering predecessors so the listener can self-congratulate on being smarter than imaginary fools - it's also factually backwards. As it happens, there was a big seemingly dumb NASA expenditure before the space pen was developed. It was pencils. 

NASA paid an engineering firm to custom build them a batch of the least bad possible mechanical pencils, to minimize the problems which graphite both had and caused in spaceflight. The payment was mostly NRE for engineering and custom machining, but $4400 over a batch of 34 worked out to 129 1965 dollars per pencil, causing a scandal. This is of course still a factor of a thousand or more less than the myth would have you believe.

Once it was developed with one million of private investment, both NASA and the Soviets paid $2.39 per space pen. The general public could too, if they bought enough of them at once. It's manufactured and sold to anyone who wants one - and widely used in spaceflight - to this day.

author: @ThatRobHuman
text: I both adore and loathe that NASA pen anecdote. I love it because it can be cautionary tale about government over-spending - and you've added to its strengths with your telling of the dichotomy between engineering approaches brilliantly. Nonetheless, I hate it because the fact that graphite is conductive, and a fine mist of conductive dust in zero-G inside of the a sensitive vehicle that is keeping you alive in the cold hard vacuum of space is something that a lot of people overlook. In my mind, that anecdotal story is equally as much about knowing when the simple solution won't actually do - a parallel to the phrase "OSHA regulations are written in blood." Granted, that particular aspect of the Pen anecdote isn't really relevant to the point you're making - but still, I see a lot of people repeat that story without thinking it through.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's fair enough mate and you are right that does happen - I take your point - cheers

replies.author: @ThatRobHuman
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering naw, this wasn't meant as calling you out or anything - it was still a good introductory hook to your premise.

The other thing about that story is that it's actually not true. Fisher ended up developing it with private capital and sold it to both NASA and the USSR.

author: @FuhrChris
text:
Could your CVT's purpose be inverted?
What I'm thinking of building would be a bike trailer that has the parts from a Coleman mini bike (a gas engined and will not go faster than 20 miles an hour without being hot rodded.) (For one to up the torque so that it wouldn't bog out the sprocket's could be changed for more force, resulting a need for more RPM's to even maintain the 20 miles an hour and even more RPM's on the motor to get it into the 45Km zone.)
The application would be be that I would run the gas engine on the trailer just to get me up a several mile long hill while I have cargo weight.
Once on top of the Mountain it's about 60 miles of flat lands and once up to speed you don't need a lot of energy to stay at speed, so the drive wheel could be raised while in motion and the gas engine could be turned off 

The bike would have both pedal and electric power.

The problem is trying to calibrate the force of the gas engine (re-geared to a lower speed so as to give it more hill climbing abilities) that would need to be mated/matched to the power of the electric motor that would be on the bike.

In short IF the pivot rod were allowed to free roam would it just fall into a position that matched the wheel speed of the other vehicle (a class 3 E-bike here cannot go faster than 28 miles an hour 45Km (If you stop peddling your legal speed is only 20 miles an hour) so I'm not sure that the CTV would suffer much from extreme forces?

If some other ground contact wheel (from some other power drive shaft/system was creating the pace and the pivot pin was able to move to the correct location automatically the remaining issue in the Math would be an outcome of torque.  The system would self adjust so that If you slowed down then you gain torque, which if all it was for was climbing a long steep hill it would work?

author: @TerryClarkAccordioncrazy
text: This is very interesting and thought provoking. By transforming to linear motion then rectifying it back to circular motion you do get a true CVT but only if the steps on your ratchet are infinitesimally small. In reality they are not which means (I think) that you will still end up with discrete gears and steps. Maybe I'm wrong and I'm not a good enough mathematician to figure it out.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: There is always a difference between the theory of a thing and the practice of a thing mate - in theory - you are quite right - in practice you don't need to go infinitesimally small the achieve a result indistinguishable from theory in use

replies.author: @TerryClarkAccordioncrazy
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering Yes you are right. I wonder where the tradeoff lies. As a cyclist I dream of a true CVT. As a scooter rider I enjoy the benefits of a true CVT but my engine bears the inefficiency. I guess racing gives the answer. Whatever the tour winner uses is optimal

author: @bruinjim1
text: The pen and pencil thing is a myth.  Early space programs used pencils, but there were problems.  The graphite would break off and could get into eyes.  And it's flammable.  The space pen was invented in 1965 by Paul Fisher and the Fisher Pen Company.  (With no funding from NASA.)  The Fisher Pen Company offered them to NASA, who bought them and started using them in 1967.  Even the soviets used them in their space missions.

author: @evanbarnes9984
text:
This is extremely cool! The idea of an actually robust CVT is incredibly exciting.

NASA used the expensive fancy space pen instead of a pencil because the loose graphite shavings from sharpening pencils might have floated around the cabin of the space craft and could have caused serious problems for the electronics. The Soviet program used a pencil. I love that old story, but I think it speaks more to differences in safety cultures than it does to differences in design philosophy. After all, the Soviet space program was a lot more deadly than ours.

replies.author: @peterfleming170
replies.text: yes agreed, carbon bits in a space craft in zero G is far too risky. They could float all over and short out all sorts of key equipment.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @maxinehardy9411
replies.text:
the thing is, even the soviets used space pens, and even nasa used pencils. and the space pen wasnt even funded by nasa, rather it was a private endeavor by paul fisher, who developed the space pen with no government funding. nasa did attempt to develop their own space pen prior to fishers, but it was prohibitively expensive, and so they abandoned it and went back to mechanical pencils despite the danger that the graphite posed. the soviets, on the other hand, used grease pencils. after the fisher space pen was finished, both the soviets and nasa adopted it very quickly, and they were sold for $2.95 a piece.

personally, rather than speaking to differences in design philosophy, or differences in safety culture, it speaks more to the fact that humans like fun anecdotes with snappy punchlines

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @maxinehardy9411 lol - it does indeed lol

replies.author: @SvenRötters
replies.text:

I bet you forgot the two accidents with the Space Shuttle.

author: @Ron-yj4rh
text: Hi Robert. A couple weeks ago, I made a video with a very simple drawing that shows how you would use a Whitworth Mechanism to make a bicycle CVT. The variable part of it would be moving the fulcrum, as I explain in my video. The only real fly in the soup is that some mechanism would be needed to move the fulcrum which I explain in the video. If you can use this, be my guest. The video is entitled The simplest (chainless) bicycle CVT.
